mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive Buddhism coverage addresses Hindu context (A.1), founding (A.2), Indian developments (A.3), Southeast Asia (A.7), modern era (A.8). Four noble truths and eightfold path relate to views of reality (C.1.a), eightfold path (C.1.b), nirvana (C.1.c). Theravada (C.2.a), Mahayana (C.2.b), monastic institutions (C.3.b), and ethical practices (C.3.a) are covered.
The historical Buddha relates to Hindu background (A.1), founding (A.2), sacred literature (B.1), views of reality (C.1.a), and eightfold path (C.1.b).
Buddhist ethics addresses traditional beliefs (C.1) and ethical practices (C.3.a).
Tibetan Buddhism addresses Buddhism in Tibet (A.6) and esoteric Buddhism (C.2.c).
Indian philosophy relates to Hindu background (A.1) and Buddhist philosophy (B.3).
Hinduism as Buddhism's cultural context relates to the Hindu background (A.1).
Confucianism's interaction with Buddhism relates to Buddhism in China (A.4).
Southeast Asian Buddhism relates to Buddhism in Ceylon and Southeast Asia (A.7).
Modern Japan provides context for Buddhism in Japan (A.5).
Korean history provides context for Buddhism in Korea (A.5).
Silk Road cultural exchange relates to Buddhism's spread to China (A.4).
Buddhist contemplative practices relate to Buddhist mysticism (B.4).
Ritual relates to Buddhist practices (C.3) and ceremonies (C.3.c).

Covers Buddhist monasticism in Tibet including the Dalai Lama's influence, communal organization, and the role of monasteries, directly addressing monastic institutions and the characteristics and role of the sangha.
Covers theoretical approaches to myth including structuralism and psychoanalytic interpretation, applicable to understanding Buddhist mythology's basic types, contents, and functions.

Schopenhauer's pessimistic philosophy of suffering, aesthetics, and the denial of the will to life was directly influenced by Buddhist philosophy, connecting 19th-century German idealism to Eastern philosophy and Buddhist philosophical concepts.

Covers the relationship between Buddhism and armed conflict including Sri Lanka's civil war, alongside Buddhist ethical frameworks for moderating violence, directly relevant to universal or prevalent ethical and religious practices.
